coredata.dc:title: Dual-site doping of PET-derived carbon quantum dots with alkali-earth and transition metals for adsorption of PFOS, ibuprofen, sucralose, and decabromodiphenyl oxide

coredata.dc:description: Emerging toxic pollutants (EPs) such as perfluorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS), ibuprofen (IBU), sucralose, and decabromodiphenyl oxide (BDE-209) pose significant threats to environmental and human health due to their persistence and bioaccumulation. This study investigates the efficacy of polyethylene terephthalate (PET)-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) functionalized with dual-site doping of alkali-earth (Ca, Mg) and transition (Zn, Fe) metals at graphitic and carbonyl (C=O) sites for the adsorption of these EPs. Using computational modeling and density functional theory (DFT), we analyzed the structural, electronic, and adsorption properties of pristine and metal-doped PET-CQDs. Results reveal that metal doping enhances surface area, solvent accessibility, and electronic reactivity, with Ca-O and Mg-O doping yielding the highest Connolly surface areas (299.54 Å2 and 289.11 Å2) and Fe-G reducing the HOMO-LUMO gap to 1.40 eV, improving charge transfer. Adsorption studies indicate that Fe-doped CQDs exhibit the strongest binding energies for PFOS (−5264.3 kcal/mol) and IBU (−8209.88 kcal/mol), driven by electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions, while BDE-209 shows the highest adsorption energy (−23335 kcal/mol) across all CQDs due to π-π stacking and hydrophobic effects. Sucralose displays weaker adsorption, with positive binding energies indicating limited affinity. Post-adsorption molecular dynamics highlight increased mobility in Ca-O and Zn-G CQDs, with diffusivity constants rising significantly (e.g., 0.03482 for IBU on Ca-O), while Fe-G and Mg-O CQDs show rigidity, reflecting stronger pollutant retention. Proposed mechanisms involve ion–dipole, electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, and π-π stacking interactions tailored by metal type and doping site. These findings elucidate structure–property relationships, demonstrating that dual-site metal doping enhances the selectivity and efficiency of PET-CQDs, offering a sustainable approach for designing advanced adsorbents for water treatment applications.

Surface area and volume of the CQDs.

3-Dimensional diagram of the HOMO-LUMO electrons distribution of the different CQDs. The difference in electron densities is demonstrated by the blue and yellow isosurfaces, which show electron loss in the yellow areas and electron accumulation in the blue areas.

Structural and dynamics analysis of CQDs before adsorption of EPs showing (a) temperature fluctuations, (b) radial distribution function (c) X-ray scattering, (d) mean square displacement (e) dipole autocorrelation function and (f) dipole autocorrelation function power spectrum.
Diffusivity coefficient for all CQDs in water.
Top view (a) and adsorption energy (b) of PFOS, IBU, sucralose and BDE-209 onto CQDs.

Linear relationship analysis between PET-CQDs structural and chemical properties and their adsorption energies for PFOS, IBU, Sucralose and BDE-209. DACF power spectrum for pollutants after adsorption by pristine and metal-doped PET-CQDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Emerging toxic pollutants (EPs) such as perfluorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS), ibuprofen (IBU), sucralose, and decabromodiphenyl oxide (BDE-209) pose significant threats to environmental and human health due to their persistence and bioaccumulation. This study investigates the efficacy of polyethylene terephthalate (PET)-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) functionalized with dual-site doping of alkali-earth (Ca, Mg) and transition (Zn, Fe) metals at graphitic and carbonyl (C=O) sites for the adsorption of these EPs. Using computational modeling and density functional theory (DFT), we analyzed the structural, electronic, and adsorption properties of pristine and metal-doped PET-CQDs. Results reveal that metal doping enhances surface area, solvent accessibility, and electronic reactivity, with Ca-O and Mg-O doping yielding the highest Connolly surface areas (299.54 Å2 and 289.11 Å2) and Fe-G reducing the HOMO-LUMO gap to 1.40 eV, improving charge transfer. Adsorption studies indicate that Fe-doped CQDs exhibit the strongest binding energies for PFOS (−5264.3 kcal/mol) and IBU (−8209.88 kcal/mol), driven by electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions, while BDE-209 shows the highest adsorption energy (−23335 kcal/mol) across all CQDs due to π-π stacking and hydrophobic effects. Sucralose displays weaker adsorption, with positive binding energies indicating limited affinity. Post-adsorption molecular dynamics highlight increased mobility in Ca-O and Zn-G CQDs, with diffusivity constants rising significantly (e.g., 0.03482 for IBU on Ca-O), while Fe-G and Mg-O CQDs show rigidity, reflecting stronger pollutant retention. Proposed mechanisms involve ion–dipole, electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, and π-π stacking interactions tailored by metal type and doping site. These findings elucidate structure–property relationships, demonstrating that dual-site metal doping enhances the selectivity and efficiency of PET-CQDs, offering a sustainable approach for designing advanced adsorbents for water treatment applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data added to the work and the supplementary files

ce:section.ce:para: Emerging toxic pollutants (EPs) represent a growing threat to environment and human health. These pollutants encompass a diverse range of chemicals that are increasingly used in modern industries, yet remain largely unregulated[19]. The lack of comprehensive regulation hinders the execution of large-scale epidemiological studies, leaving significant gaps in our understanding of their long-term impact on ecosystems and public health. EPs include compounds such as ibuprofen (IBU), a widely consumed pharmaceutical; perfluorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS), a member of the persistent Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS); sucralose, a commonly used artificial sweetener; and decabromodiphenyl oxide (BDE-209) a common polybrominated diphenyl ethers (PBDEs), which are key components of Brominated Flame Retardants (BFRs). These pollutants are often detected in aquatic and terrestrial environments due to their high resistance to degradation, leading to bioaccumulation and adverse effects on wildlife and human health [45]. Traditional methods for detecting and removing EPs from the environment, such as advanced oxidation processes and enzymatic degradation, face limitations including high costs, operational complexity, and reduced efficiency for certain pollutant classes. As a result, the development of alternative, cost-effective, and robust sensing materials for detecting and adsorbing EPs has become a priority in environmental research[9,11].

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), synthesized from polyethylene terephthalate (PET) waste, offer a sustainable and versatile platform for addressing this challenge. PET-derived CQDs (PET-CQDs) are not only environmentally friendly but also possess unique properties, including high surface area, excellent water solubility, and abundant functional groups that make them highly suitable for sensing and adsorption applications[9,11,9]. The valorization of PET waste into high-value materials aligns with circular economy principles while addressing critical environmental challenges. However, the adsorption capacity and selectivity of pristine CQDs often require enhancement to achieve practical application standards.

Metal doping has shown considerable potential in modifying the electronic and surface properties of carbon-based materials[23]. The strategic incorporation of metals at multiple sites can create synergistic effects that enhance adsorption performance. The functionalization of CQDs with metals such as alkali-earth metals (e.g., calcium and magnesium) and transition metals (e.g., zinc and iron) can further enhances their surface reactivity and selectivity. Specifically, doping these metals onto the graphitic and carbonyl (C=O) groups of PET-CQDs can significantly improve their adsorption capabilities toward EPs through enhanced metal–ligand interactions and π-π stacking.

This study employs computational methods to investigate the dual-site doping of PET-CQDs with alkali-earth metals (Ca and Mg) and transition metals (Zn and Fe) at two critical surface sites: graphitic structures and carbonyl groups. This approach aims to leverage the distinct chemical properties of these metal species to create tailored binding sites for different classes of contaminants. Through computational modeling and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, we provide detailed insights into the electronic structure modifications, binding energies, and adsorption mechanisms of the metal-doped CQDs with selected EPs—ibuprofen, PFOS, sucralose, and BDE-209. By comparing the adsorption performance of functionalized PET-CQDs, this work aims to establish structure–property relationships that can guide the rational design of more effective adsorbent materials. Our computational approach enables the systematic evaluation of factors influencing adsorption performance, including the role of metal type, doping configuration, and pollutant characteristics. This research contributes to the fundamental understanding of metal-doped carbon nanomaterials for environmental applications while offering practical insights for the development of enhanced water treatment technologies. The findings may pave the way for the design of more efficient and selective adsorbents for the simultaneous removal of diverse water pollutants.
All computational analysis was conducted in BIOVIA material studio Dassault Systèmes software.

In our experimental study, PET-CQDs were synthesized and analyzed using ATR-FTIR spectroscopy. The results (Fig. S1) revealed surface functional groups predominantly comprising carboxylic (–COOH), carbonyl (C=O), hydroxyl (–OH), and stacked aromatic rings forming a graphitic core. Based on this analysis, a 3D atomistic model of PET-CQDs was developed to represent the graphitic base with attached functional groups, including carbonyl (C=O) and carboxylic (–COOH) groups. For this study, specific sites on the PET-CQDs were selected for doping, namely the graphitic site (carbon 16) and the carbonyl group (oxygen on carbon 12). These sites were functionalized with alkali-earth metals (calcium, Ca and magnesium, Mg) and transition metals (zinc, Zn and iron, Fe). The optimized structures of the doped PET-CQDs are presented in Fig. 1 , with the following configurations:
•
Ca-G_PET-CQDs: Calcium doped at the graphitic site of the PET-CQDs.
•
Ca-O_PET-CQDs: Calcium doped at the carbonyl site of the PET-CQDs.
•
Mg-G_PET-CQDs: Magnesium doped at the graphitic site of the PET-CQDs.
•
Mg-O_PET-CQDs: Magnesium doped at the carbonyl site of the PET-CQDs.
•
Zn-G_PET-CQDs: Zinc doped at the graphitic site of the PET-CQDs.
•
Zn-O_PET-CQDs: Zinc doped at the carbonyl site of the PET-CQDs.
•
Fe-G_PET-CQDs: Iron doped at the graphitic site of the PET-CQDs.
•
Fe-O_PET-CQDs: Iron doped at the carbonyl site of the PET-CQDs.

These modeled and optimized structures formed the basis for further analysis of their adsorption capabilities and interactions with EPs. However, the EPs i.e IBU (C13H18O2, PubChem ID: 3672), PFOS (C8HF17O3S, PubChem ID: 74483), sucralose (C12H19Cl3O8, PubChem ID: 71485), and Decabromodiphenyl oxide (BDE-209; C12Br10O, PubChem ID: 14410) structures were downloaded as structure data file from PubChem, optimized and used as the adsorbates.

The structures atom volumes and surfaces were analyzed for Cornolly and accessible solvent surfaces. The cornolly model grid resolution and interval were set to coarse and 0.75 Å respectively. Additionally, the van der Waals scale factor and cornolly radius were set to 1 Å while the isosurfaces were default with 1.4 Å. Furthermore, quantum chemical analysis was carried out by geometry optimization of the structures in water (using COSMO model) employing the density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional level of theory in DMol3+ server. The GGA/PBE level of theory was chosen for its ability to balance computational efficiency and accuracy in describing the exchange–correlation energy, particularly for systems involving weak interactions such as van der Waals forces, which are important in adsorption studies [7,8]. The PBE functional, a well-established variant of GGA, was specifically selected due to its reliable performance in capturing electronic and geometric properties of materials, especially for transition metal complexes and systems involving π-π stacking interactions[31]. The conductor-like screening model (COSMO) was used to simulate the aqueous environment, ensuring a more realistic representation of the adsorption processes under experimental conditions. The COSMO model approximates the dielectric properties of water (set to 78.54), accounting for solvent effects on the electronic structure and adsorption properties of the studied systems[39]. Incorporating the solvation model is essential, as EPs like IBU, PFOS, sucralose, and BDE typically exist in aquatic ecosystems, reflects the real-world scenario where these interactions occur.

Key quantum parameters, such as the energies of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), energy gap (Egap) (Eq. (1), electronic chemical potential (μ) (Eq. (2), electronegativity (χ) (Eq. (2), ionisation potential (IE) (Eq. (3), and electron affinity (EA) (Eq. (4), were determined by analysing the optimised structure of CQDs. These metrics offer important information about the PET-CQDs' electrical and reactive characteristics.

Molecular adsorption and dynamics simulations were conducted using the “Adsorption Locator” and “Forcite” modules respectively in Material Studio. For molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, a smart algorithm was utilized to minimize the simulation system, system charged using an equilibration method (Qeq) under a universal forcefield and dynamics were performed within the NVT ensemble at a system temperature of 300 K with Nose control method (Nose mass parameter, Q mass = 0.500775 kcal/mol*ps2). The relaxation time was set to 0.1 ps. Non-bonded interactions, including van der Waals and electrostatic forces, were calculated using atom-based methods and Ewald summation with an accuracy of 0.001 kcal/mol. A cutoff distance of 12.5 A was applied across all systems. The molecular dynamics simulation was run for 100 ps with a time step of 1 fs. The following formula was ultimately used to calculate the interaction energies after the MD simulation of the EPs adsorption using the Forcite module:

Furthermore, the output of the before and after adsorption were analyzed including the temperature fluctuations, radial distribution function, X-ray scattering, dipole autocorrelation function and power spectrum.

For the analysis of atomic spatial distribution in molecular dynamics simulations, radial distribution functions (RDFs), often referred to as pair correlation functions, are essential tools[37]. The RDF, represented as g(r), measures the likelihood of discovering a certain atom pair i, j, within a spherical shell of thickness dr, separated by a radial distance r. Equation (9), which describes the normalised RDF expression, provides a mathematical description of this connection.

A popular method for examining structural characteristics in solution and locating structural transitions brought on by adsorption processes is small-and wide-angle X-ray scattering, or SWAXS. In this work, the structural alterations in the various PET-FCQDs before to and during the adsorption of EPs were examined using SWAXS measurements. The differences in scattering intensity between the baseline PET-FCQDs simulation (A) and a second simulation with adsorbed EP molecules (B) were highlighted by using MD simulations to calculate the scattered X-ray intensity (I) as a function of the scattering vector (2θ), taking into account energy changes (ω) as explained in equation (10). This provided important insights into structural modifications caused by adsorption[9,11]. i.e,

The MSD is a term used in statistical mechanics to describe how a particle's position changes over time in relation to a reference point[7,8]. It may be thought of as the area of the framework “investigated” by the random walker (an arbitrary interaction that portrays a manner that incorporates a succession of irregular steps over some numerical space) and is the most often used measure of the spatial degree of an arbitrary movement. ce:section.ce:section-title: Dipole autocorrelation power spectrum and function
ce:section.ce:para:
One essential technique for examining the infrared spectra of adsorbed molecules is the dipole autocorrelation function. It offers insights into the dynamic interactions within the system and is crucial for comprehending spectral line broadening. The rapid Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function of dipole moments is used to compute the infrared spectra[38,9,11]:

All data obtained were analyzed and plotted with aid of origin software.

The surface area and volume characteristics of the PET-derived CQDs (PET-CQDs) and their metal-doped derivatives are presented in Fig. 2 (summarized in Table S1), providing important information into their structural changes upon functionalization. These parameters are critical in determining the adsorption capacity and interaction potential with the EPs. The Connolly surface area and surface-occupied volume represent the accessible surface regions of the CQDs (Fig. 2a). The PET-CQDs have a Connolly surface area of 277.10 Å2 and a Connolly surface-occupied volume of 266.69 Å3, which slightly increase upon metal doping (except for Zn-G). The Connolly surface area and volume increase for all dopants at the graphitic sites (Ca-G, Mg-G, Zn-G, Fe-G) with the highest values observed for Mg doping (290.08 Å2 and 279.20 Å3). This increase indicates a structural modification of the graphitic site, which may improve the accessibility for pollutant interactions. However, the carbonyl site (Ca-O, Mg-O, Zn-O, Fe-O) doping results in a slightly higher increase in surface area and volume compared to graphitic doping (except for Fe), particularly for Ca (299.54 Å2 and 283.98 Å3) and Mg (289.11 Å2 and 287.22 Å3) (Fig. 2a). This suggests that doping at the carbonyl group may result in better dispersion and accessibility for adsorption sites.

The accessible solvent surface area (SASA) and occupied volume provide information into the interaction of the CQDs with surrounding aqueous environments. Metal-doped CQDs exhibit higher SASA compared to pristine PET-CQDs (489.50 Å2), with the most significant increases observed for Ca-O (525.55 Å2) and Mg-O (506.55 Å2) doped structures (Fig. 2b). This indicates enhanced wettability[32]and a higher likelihood of interaction with pollutants in solution. Similarly, the accessible solvent-occupied volume increases for all doped structures, suggesting that metal doping increases the effective interaction space. For instance, Ca-O doping results in the largest accessible solvent-occupied volume (866.66 Å3), highlighting its potential for adsorbing bulky pollutants. The COSMO surface area and cavity volume provide information about the electrostatic interaction potential of the CQDs in an aqueous medium [10]. The COSMO surface area of the doped CQDs increases across all dopants compared to the pristine PET-CQDs (294.86 Å2) are presented in Fig. 2c. Notably, Ca-O and Mg-O doping result in the largest COSMO surface areas (321.80 Å2 and 302.79 Å2, respectively), suggesting improved electronic properties conducive to adsorption [10]. The COSMO total cavity volume also increases slightly for doped structures, with Ca-O showing the most pronounced change (346.60 Å3). This indicates enhanced availability of internal cavities for pollutant trapping[35]. The results suggest that doping at the C=O generally provides a greater increase in surface area, volume, and solvent accessibility compared to doping at the graphitic site. Among the dopants, calcium (Ca-O) and magnesium (Mg-O) exhibit the most significant increases in both solvent-accessible properties and COSMO surface areas. These enhancements indicate their potential for higher adsorption capacities and stronger interactions with both hydrophobic charged or polar EPs.

The total energy values reflect the stability of the CQDs in their optimized geometries. The PET-CQDs exhibit the highest (least negative) total energy of −1028.32 Ha, indicating lower stability compared to the doped structures. The most stable structures are Zn-O_PET-CQDs and Zn-G_PET-CQDs with total energies of −2807.56 Ha and −2807.36 Ha, respectively. This suggests that zinc doping significantly stabilizes the CQDs, particularly at the C=O site. The total energy obtained in this study are similar for DFT total energy reported in transition metal-doped platinum CQDs,[3].

Binding energy values reveal the strength of interaction between the dopant and the CQD. PET-CQDs have a binding energy of −4329.89 kcal/mol, which becomes more negative upon doping (except for Ca doping at C=O, −3765.1 kcal/mol), indicating stronger interactions. The strongest binding is observed for Fe-O_PET-CQDs (−4492.97 kcal/mol), highlighting the high affinity of Fe at the C=O site.

DFT calculations play a fundamental role in understanding and predicting the reactivity of molecular species, particularly organic molecules. A key component of these calculations is Frontier Molecular Orbital (FMO) theory, which examines the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) of a system[6]. The HOMO represents the highest energy level occupied by electrons, while the LUMO is the lowest available energy level that can accept electrons. The energy gap between these orbitals, known as the HOMO-LUMO gap, is a crucial indicator of a molecule’s chemical reactivity, stability, and electronic properties[7,8]. A smaller HOMO-LUMO gap corresponds to higher reactivity, as it requires less energy for an electron transition from the HOMO (electron donor) to the LUMO (electron acceptor). The results of this study, summarized in Table 1, provide a theoretical evaluation of the energy gaps for both pristine and metal-doped PET-CQDs, while the corresponding orbital densities are depicted in Fig. 3 . The pristine PET-CQDs exhibit a HOMO energy of −5.22 eV and a LUMO energy of −3.43 eV, yielding an energy gap of 1.79 eV, which serves as the baseline for comparison. Upon metal doping, subtle variations in the energy gap are observed. Specifically, Fe-G_PET-CQDs and Mg-O_PET-CQDs exhibit reduced energy gaps of 1.40 eV and 1.55 eV, respectively, suggesting that these dopants enhance the electronic reactivity of the CQDs. This reduction in energy gap improves charge transfer potential, which is particularly important for adsorption applications, as it facilitates interactions between CQDs and pollutant molecules[9,11]. Similar energy gap variations have been reported in transition metal-doped platinum CQDs, where the HOMO-LUMO gap ranged from 1.78 eV to 2.75 eV[3]. These findings align with the present study, reinforcing the influence of metal doping on charge transfer capabilities and overall reactivity.

Electronic chemical potential (μ) represents a molecule’s tendency to donate electrons, while electronegativity (X) reflects its ability to attract electrons [7]. PET-CQDs exhibit a chemical potential of −0.90 eV, which becomes slightly less negative after doping, except for Ca-G (−0.91 eV), Mg-G (−0.93 eV), and Zn-O (−1.02 eV). This trend suggests that most metal-doped PET-CQDs have an enhanced electron-donating ability, making them more reactive for pollutant interactions. The electronegativity of PET-CQDs is 0.90 eV, which slightly decreases upon doping, except for Ca-G (0.91 eV), Mg-G (0.93 eV), and Zn-O (1.02 eV). The increased electronegativity in these cases suggests a greater ability to accept electrons, improving interactions with electron-rich pollutants. This behavior aligns with previous studies on metal-doped carbon materials, where metal incorporation subtly adjusts the charge transfer efficiency [17,5].

Ionization potential (IE) measures the energy required to remove an electron, while electron affinity (EA) reflects a material’s ability to accept electrons. PET-CQDs exhibit an IE of 5.22 eV, which slightly decreases for most doped structures, except for those doped with Zn. The lowest IE is observed in Mg-O_PET-CQDs (5.01 eV), indicating that these CQDs require less energy for electron removal, improving their interaction with pollutants. Lower IE values correlate with increased adsorption efficiency, as reported in similar DFT studies on graphene-based materials[30,29]. For EA, PET-CQDs have a value of 3.43 eV, which remains fairly stable across doped CQDs, except for Zn-O_PET-CQDs, which exhibit the highest EA (3.56 eV). The increased EA in Zn-O_PET-CQDs suggests a stronger ability to accept electrons, making them particularly effective for capturing electron-rich pollutants, such as aromatic organic pollutants.

Global hardness (η) quantifies a material’s resistance to deformation or polarization, while softness (S) measures its ability to distribute electron density flexibly. A lower hardness suggests greater adaptability for adsorption, while higher softness indicates better pollutant interaction potential. PET-CQDs exhibit a hardness of 0.90 eV, with doped structures showing slight variations. Notably, Fe-G_PET-CQDs have the lowest hardness (0.70 eV), meaning they are more chemically adaptable, making them ideal for pollutant adsorption. PET-CQDs have an initial softness of 1.12 eV, which increases for most doped CQDs, except for Ca, Mg-O, and Zn-O. Among all doped structures, Fe-O_PET-CQDs exhibit the highest softness (1.32 eV), implying that these CQDs have superior charge transfer capabilities, making them highly effective in pollutant removal.
The molecular dynamic results of the CQDs before adsorption of EPs is presented in Fig. 4 .

One important metric for assessing if a model system has achieved equilibrium is the temperature equilibrium criterion. Temperature variations must stabilise between 5 % and 10 % in order to reach equilibrium. After 10 ps, the temperature changes seen throughout the adsorption dynamics in this investigation (Fig. 4a) were within 10 %, suggesting that the simulation system had effectively achieved equilibrium. In order to guarantee the precision and dependability of the computational outcomes, equilibrium must be reached [7].

The RDF, g(r), provides information into the structural arrangement and atomic interactions within a material by describing how atomic density varies as a function of distance (r) from a reference atom[18]. The RDF plot shows pristine PET-CQDs and various metal-doped PET-CQDs (Ca, Mg, Zn, and Fe in both G and O configurations) before adsorption (Fig. 4b). The first prominent peaks appear between 1.0 Å and 2.0 Å, indicating the short-range atomic interactions within PET-CQDs and their metal-doped derivatives. The intensity and position of these peaks vary across different doping configurations, reflecting how metal doping alters the local bonding environment and atomic structure. Secondary peaks extend beyond 2.0 Å, gradually decreasing, which signifies longer-range atomic correlations that weaken with increasing distance (Fig. 4b). Pristine PET-CQDs display well-defined peaks, indicating a stable and ordered atomic structure before adsorption. Metal-doped PET-CQDs exhibit similar peak patterns suggesting that metal incorporation did not significantly affects interatomic distances and structural rigidity. RDF analysis before adsorption serves as a baseline to assess how the structure evolves after pollutant adsorption.

The XRS pattern explains the structural characteristics, crystallinity, and phase composition of PET-CQDs and their metal-doped variants before adsorption[7]. The intensity (I) vs. 2-theta (°) plot illustrates the scattering behavior of pristine PET-CQDs and various metal-doped PET-CQDs, including Ca, Mg, Zn, and Fe in both G and O configurations (Fig. 4c). The broad features observed in the X-ray scattering spectra indicate that both pristine and doped PET-CQDs exhibit an amorphous or semi-crystalline structure[26]. The broad peaks suggest a lack of long-range periodic atomic ordering, which is characteristic of CQDs[16]. The gradual intensity decay with increasing 2-theta values is a common feature of disordered carbonaceous materials, reinforcing the presence of randomly oriented graphitic domains. Compared to pristine PET-CQDs, the doped samples show slight variations in peak intensity and position, which suggests that metal doping influences the local structural arrangement (Fig. 4c). Among the doped CQDs, Zn-O and Fe-G PET-CQDs exhibit the highest initial intensity, implying a stronger scattering effect. This may indicate enhanced atomic interactions and increased electron density contrast due to metal incorporation, which can be observed in the global softness data (Table 1 ). In contrast, Zn-G, Ca-G and Mg-G PET-CQDs show relatively lower intensity values, suggesting that these dopants induce minor structural modifications without significantly altering the overall amorphous nature of the PET-CQDs. The shift in intensity profiles among doped samples suggest modifications in interatomic distances and localized distortions in the CQD framework. Differences between graphene- (G) and oxygen-functionalized (O) doped PET-CQDs indicate that oxygen-rich environments may enhance structural stabilization. This is particularly evident in Zn-O and Fe-O PET-CQDs, which exhibit slightly higher scattering intensity at 12.5 (2-theta°). The observed X-ray scattering behavior aligns with previous studies on metal-doped carbon nanomaterials, where metal incorporation enhances structural integrity and surface reactivity[41].

MSD is a key parameter in molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, indicating how far atoms or molecules move over time, thus reflecting diffusivity and structural flexibility[15,34]. The MSD plot explains the dynamics and mobility of PET-CQDs and their metal-doped counterparts before adsorption (Fig. 4d). In the graph (Fig. 4d), the pristine PET-CQDs show a moderate increase in MSD over time, suggesting a stable structure with limited atomic movement. Upon doping, variations in MSD values are observed, indicating that different metal dopants influence the mobility and flexibility of the PET-CQDs. Among the doped structures, Ca-G_PET-CQDs, Zn-G_PET-CQDs and Zn-O_PET-CQDs exhibit the highest MSD values, particularly after 80 ps, suggesting greater atomic displacement and enhanced structural flexibility. This increased mobility indicates that Ca-G and Zn-O doping introduce weaker interatomic interactions, allowing atoms to move more freely. This behavior can be advantageous for adsorption applications, as higher flexibility may facilitate better pollutant interaction and surface adaptability. In contrast, Mg-O_PET-CQDs and Fe-O_PET-CQDs exhibit the lowest MSD values, indicating greater structural rigidity and reduced atomic mobility. This suggests that Mg-O and Fe-O doping strengthen the PET-CQD framework, possibly due to stronger bonding and enhanced structural stabilization. Such behavior aligns with literature reports indicating that metal-doped carbon structures can exhibit varying degrees of lattice rigidity, depending on the nature of the dopant and its interaction with the carbon network[25]. The remaining doped structures, such as Fe-G, and Ca-O PET-CQDs, exhibit moderate MSD values, suggesting an intermediate balance between rigidity and flexibility. This indicates that their atomic movement is partially constrained, which may be beneficial for maintaining structural integrity while still allowing interaction with pollutants.

From the MSD, the diffusivity constant (D) for the CQDs were computed and presented in Fig. 5 . The diffusivity constant reflects the ease with which the nanoparticles diffuse in an aqueous medium, and it is generally influenced by their size, shape, and interactions with the surrounding water molecules[7,30]. The pristine PET-CQDs exhibit a relatively low D values, indicating slower diffusion in water. This is likely due to their inherent structural characteristics, including relatively larger effective hydrodynamic size and strong interactions with water molecules[43]). Among the doped CQDs, Ca-G_PET-CQDs exhibit the highest diffusivity constant, suggesting that Ca doping enhances the mobility of PET-CQDs in water, possibly due to changes in surface characteristics, such as reduced hydrodynamic radius or weakened interactions with water molecules. The increased diffusion rate for Ca-G_PET-CQDs may make them advantageous for applications where high mobility is required, such as in catalytic or drug delivery systems[14]. Zn-O_PET-CQDs and Mg-G_PET-CQDs also show relatively higher diffusivity compared to other doped CQDs. This suggests that Zn-G and Mg-G doping improve the dynamic properties of PET-CQDs. The D values followed the order; Ca-G_PET-CQDs > Zn-O_PET-CQDs > Zn-G_PET-CQDs > Mg-G_PET-CQDs > Fe-G_PET-CQDs > Ca-O_PET-CQDs > Fe-O_PET-CQDs > Mg-O_PET-CQDs.

DACF represents the correlation of molecular dipoles over time and reflects the rotational motion and stability of dipoles in the system[22]. The DACF of the CQDs are presented in Fig. 4e. A higher DACF value indicates strong dipole retention, while a faster decay suggests increased rotational freedom and dipole relaxation[9,11]. The pristine PET-CQDs maintain a high DACF value of approximately 0.98–1.00 throughout the simulation. This indicates slow dipole decorrelation and a relatively rigid dipole orientation over time, suggesting that the pristine PET-CQDs have a stable dipole alignment. This stability could influence their interaction with adsorbates, making them effective in applications requiring consistent dipolar interactions[36]. The Fe-G_PET-CQDs, Zn-O_PET-CQDs, Ca-G_PET-CQDs and Mg-G_PET-CQDs also exhibit high DACF values, indicating strong dipole retention and minimal loss of dipole orientation, suggesting enhanced structural stability and dipolar persistence. In contrast, Mg-O_PET-CQDs and Ca-O_PET-CQDs exhibit greater fluctuations and faster DACF decay, implying that their dipole orientations are more dynamic and undergo faster relaxation suggesting that these doped PET-CQDs experience increased rotational freedom, which could be linked to weaker dipole interactions or enhanced flexibility. While this may reduce long-term dipole stability, it could improve adsorption efficiency by allowing more dynamic molecular interactions. The Fe-O_PET-CQDs show the most significant DACF drop, reaching values as low as 0.65. This indicates rapid dipole decorrelation and increased rotational mobility, meaning that Fe-O doping reduces the persistence of dipole orientation. This behavior could be due to weaker dipole–dipole interactions or structural distortions introduced by Fe-O doping, making these PET-CQDs more flexible but potentially less stable.

The DACF power spectrum (Fig. 4f) elucidates the vibrational characteristics of CQDs, showing a consistent pattern of an intense low-frequency peak followed by rapid decay[38]. Variations arise from metal type and doping site. Fe-O_PET-CQDs exhibit the strongest initial peak with swift decay and few secondary features, indicating simple, robust vibrational modes. Mg-doped CQDs, especially Mg-O, display complex spectra with secondary peaks at 20–40 cm-1, suggesting additional modes from metal-surface interactions . Ca-doped variants show intermediate behavior, with Ca-O outperforming Ca-G in peak intensity and mid-frequency structure, while Zn-doped CQDs exhibit modest peaks and minimal secondary features, hinting at localized effects. Oxygen-site doping consistently yields higher-intensity peaks and broader frequency distributions than graphitic-site doping, reflecting stronger dipole fluctuations and more vibrational complexity [28]. Compared to pristine PET-CQDs, which show peaks up to 40 cm-1, metal doping significantly alters vibrational properties, with metal type and site as key determinants.

The adsorption of EPs onto CQDs is a complex process influenced by the structural properties, functionalization, and electronic characteristics of the CQDs. The molecular adsorption poses (top view) and adsorption energies of EPs onto PET-CQDs are presented in Fig. 6 a and 6b, while detailed adsorption parameters, including total energy, rigid adsorption energy, deformation energy, and differential adsorption energy, are summarized in Table S2.

The adsorption energy (Eads​) values provide insights into the strength of interaction between the EPs and PET-CQDs. A more negative adsorption energy corresponds to stronger adsorption, which directly correlates with pollutant removal efficiency[7,8]. Among all tested pollutants, BDE-209 exhibits the strongest adsorption onto PET-CQDs, with Eads values ranging from −23314 to −23335 kcal/mol. This strong interaction can be attributed to the large molecular structure and high affinity of BDE-209 for the graphitic core and oxygen-containing functional groups on CQDs as well as the low energy gaps (1.40 eV to 2.04 eV) of the CQDs (Fig. 3). Similarly, sucralose demonstrates high adsorption affinity (∼−17367 kcal/mol), likely due to its polar functional groups, which interact favorably with the oxygen-rich CQD surface. In contrast, PFOS exhibits moderate adsorption (∼−2561 kcal/mol), while IBU shows the weakest interaction (∼−21 kcal/mol). While metal doping does not drastically alter the overall adsorption energy trends, it enhances interactions in specific cases. For instance, Ca-O and Zn-G PET-CQDs show slightly stronger adsorption for BDE-209 and PFOS compared to pristine PET-CQDs. This enhancement can be attributed to the increased binding sites introduced by metal functionalization, which improves pollutant affinity (Fig. 6b and Table S2). Rigid adsorption energy quantifies the direct interaction between the EPs and CQDs without accounting for structural changes, whereas deformation energy represents the structural modifications that occur upon adsorption[9,11]. The results indicate that BDE-209 and sucralose exhibit high deformation energy values (∼−23301 kcal/mol and ∼−17353 kcal/mol, respectively). This suggests significant restructuring of CQDs upon adsorption, which may contribute to stronger interactions and enhanced pollutant retention. Conversely, PFOS and IBU exhibit lower deformation energy values (∼−2550 kcal/mol and ∼−8.6 kcal/mol, respectively), implying minimal structural rearrangement upon adsorption. This indicates that PFOS primarily interacts through surface binding without significantly altering the CQD structure, whereas IBU has weaker interactions that do not induce major CQD modifications (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of metal doping on adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: Metal doping plays a crucial role in modifying the electronic properties and surface reactivity of PET-CQDs, thereby influencing their adsorption capacity. Ca-O and Fe-O PET-CQDs demonstrate enhanced interactions with PFOS (∼−2561.26 kcal/mol), suggesting that these dopants improve affinity toward perfluorinated compounds. Additionally, Zn-O and Fe doping enhance the adsorption of IBU and sucralose, making these materials more effective for removing pharmaceuticals and artificial sweeteners. The differential adsorption energy (dEads/dNi​) closely aligns with Eads​, indicating consistent adsorption behavior regardless of pollutant concentration. This suggests that PET-CQDs maintain their adsorption efficiency even at higher pollutant concentrations, making them promising candidates for environmental remediation applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship with structural and chemical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacity of PET-CQDs is significantly influenced by their structural and chemical characteristics, which dictate their interaction with pollutants. A comprehensive analysis of the correlation between structural properties (such as Connolly surface area, Connolly surface occupied volume, accessible solvent surface area, accessible solvent surface occupied volume, Cosmo surface area of the cavity, and Cosmo total volume of the cavity) and chemical properties (including total energy, binding energy, HOMO, LUMO, and energy gap) provides insight into the adsorption behavior of both pristine and metal-doped PET-CQDs. The linear relationship between these properties and adsorption energies for PFOS, IBU, Sucralose, and BDE-209 is illustrated in Fig. 7 . Overall, the relationships between structural properties and adsorption energies were weakly positive for most pollutants (Fig. 7a-d), suggesting that variations in surface characteristics contribute moderately to adsorption efficiency. However, the accessible solvent surface area (SASA) for PFOS exhibited a stronger correlation (R2 = 0.51), indicating that solvent accessibility plays a more pronounced role in PFOS adsorption compared to other pollutants. Looking critically at the data (Fig. 2 and Table S1), metal-doped PET-CQDs generally exhibit increased solvent-accessible surface areas, which enhances pollutant adsorption. For instance, Ca-O PET-CQDs and Fe-O PET-CQDs show some of the highest Cosmo surface areas (∼321.8 Å2 and ∼ 301.57 Å2, respectively), providing additional sites for pollutant interaction. Moreover, the Connolly surface occupied volume values suggest that Ca-O and Zn-G PET-CQDs have slightly higher adsorption capacity, aligning with their observed enhancement in adsorption energies. These structural characteristics confirm that modifications at the graphitic and carbonyl sites impacts pollutant adsorption efficiency. Among the metal-doped CQDs, Ca-O, Zn-G, and Fe-O PET-CQDs show the most promising improvements in adsorption, particularly for PFOS and BDE-209. These findings highlight the potential of PET-derived CQDs as efficient and tunable adsorbents for tackling a wide range of emerging pollutants in water treatment applications. Future studies should explore further modifications, such as dual-metal doping or nitrogen functionalization, to optimize CQD performance for a broader range of contaminants.

For the quantum chemical properties (Fig. 7e), the correlation with adsorption energy varied across different pollutants. In the case of PFOS, the relationship ranged from moderate (R2 < 0.3) to strong, with LUMO exhibiting the highest correlation (R2 = 0.63), suggesting that the electronic structure, particularly the LUMO energy, plays a significant role in PFOS adsorption. However, for Ibuprofen (IBU), Sucralose, and BDE-209, the correlation was weak to negligible, with total energy, HOMO, LUMO, and energy gap showing R2 values below 0.0006, indicating that quantum chemical properties have minimal influence on the adsorption of these pollutants. This suggests that while electronic factors are important for PFOS adsorption, other mechanisms such as electrostatic interactions and surface characteristics may dominate for the remaining pollutants.

The binding energy for EPs by the different CQDs are presented in Fig. 8 . Binding energy measures the strength of interaction between CQDs and pollutants. A more negative binding energy indicates stronger adsorption, while a less negative or positive value suggests weaker interaction [10]. For PFOS adsorption, Fe-doped CQDs show the strongest binding energies (Fe-G: −5264.3 kcal/mol, Fe-O: −5251.25 kcal/mol), exceeding pristine PET-CQDs (−5088.17 kcal/mol). Ca-O_PET-CQDs exhibit the weakest interaction (−4523.21 kcal/mol), while Mg-O and Ca-G CQDs show moderate adsorption strength. Similarly, with IBU, Fe-doped variants demonstrate superior adsorption (Fe-G: −8209.88 kcal/mol, Fe-O: −8197.05 kcal/mol) compared to pristine PET-CQDs (−8034.21 kcal/mol). Ca-O_PET-CQDs again show the weakest binding (−7469.2 kcal/mol), while Zn-G and Mg-G variants perform slightly better than pristine CQDs. Sucralose and BDE-209 exhibits positive binding energies across all CQDs, indicating poor adsorption or repulsion. Ca-O_PET-CQDs (9188.165 and 16874.25 kcal/mol) and pristine PET-CQDs (8623.105 and 16308.19 kcal/mol) show the highest values for sucralose and BDE-209 respectively. The binding energy results indicate several key trends. Fe-doped CQDs (Fe-G and Fe-O) consistently show the strongest adsorption for PFOS and IBU, suggesting that Fe enhances electrostatic and chemical interactions with polar pollutants. Ca-O_PET-CQDs exhibit the weakest binding energies for PFOS and IBU, indicating reduced adsorption efficiency. Sucralose and BDE-209 adsorption is weak across all CQDs, suggesting that PET-CQDs are not highly effective for sucralose removal. These findings highlight how metal doping influences pollutant adsorption strength, allowing for the design of tailored CQDs for specific environmental remediation applications.

The figure 9 presents the radial distribution function (RDF) of PET-CQDs and metal-doped PET-CQDs after adsorption with four different pollutants: PFOS (Fig. 9a), Ibuprofen (IBU) (Fig. 9b), Sucralose (Fig. 9c), and BDE-209 (Fig. 9d). Compared to the RDF before adsorption (Fig. 4b), peak loss and additional peaks formed after adsorption was due to adsorption of these pollutants by the different PET-CQDs.

Across all subplots, distinct peaks are observed at short distances (1–3 Å), indicating strong interactions between the CQDs and pollutants particularly indicating hydrogen bonding[9,11]. However, weak peaks were formed > 3.5 Å, which were more significant in BDE-209 indicates chemical bonding. The magnitude and number of peaks vary among the different CQDs, suggesting variations in adsorption strength depending on the type of doping.

For PFOS adsorption, Fe-G_PET-CQDs and Fe-O_PET-CQDs show the highest peaks, indicating strong electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions. Zn-G/O_PET-CQDs exhibit moderate peaks, while Ca-G and Mg-G doped CQDs also demonstrate favorable PFOS interaction. IBU adsorption is strongest with Ca-G_PET-CQDs and Mg-G_PET-CQDs, likely due to coordination with IBU's functional groups. Zn-doped CQDs show moderate interaction, while Fe-O_PET-CQDs display lower adsorption. For Sucralose, Mg-G_PET-CQDs and Zn-G_PET-CQDs demonstrate significant peaks, followed by Ca-O and Ca-G_PET-CQDs. Fe-doped CQDs show comparatively weaker binding. BDE-209 adsorption is most prominent with Zn-O and Fe-O_PET-CQDs, followed by Mg-O and Ca-G_PET-CQDs, while pristine PET-CQDs show the weakest interactions. The RDF analysis reveals that metal doping significantly enhances the adsorption capability of PET-CQDs, with specific dopants exhibiting stronger interactions with certain pollutants. Fe and Zn doping are particularly effective for PFOS and BDE-209, while Ca and Mg doping enhance adsorption for IBU and Sucralose. These findings suggest that tailored doping strategies can optimize CQDs for specific environmental remediation applications, allowing for targeted pollutant removal.

The Fig. S2 presents the X-ray scattering patterns of PET-CQDs and metal-doped PET-CQDs after adsorption of four different EPs. X-ray scattering analysis provides insights into the structural changes in CQDs following pollutant adsorption, revealing modifications in crystallinity and surface interactions [7,8]. Across all subplots, the scattering intensity (I) gradually decreases as the 2-Theta value increases, indicating the amorphous nature of PET-CQDs and their metal-doped counterparts. The overall scattering intensity varies among different CQDs and pollutants, suggesting different adsorption efficiencies and structural modifications. For PFOS adsorption, Fe-O_PET-CQDs and Fe-G_PET-CQDs exhibit the highest scattering intensity. For IBU adsorption, Ca-G_PET-CQDs and Mg-G_PET-CQDs show strong scattering intensities, suggesting enhanced interaction with ibuprofen molecules. Zn-G and Zn-O_PET-CQDs also exhibit notable scattering, while Fe-O_PET-CQDs and Fe-G_PET-CQDs show relatively lower intensities, indicating weaker adsorption compared to other pollutants. For Sucralose adsorption, Mg-G_PET-CQDs and Zn-G_PET-CQDs exhibit high scattering intensities. Furthermore, Ca-O and Ca-G_PET-CQDs also show significant scattering, indicating structural changes due to adsorption. Fe-O and Fe-G doped CQDs exhibit lower scattering intensities, indicating relatively weaker adsorption. For BDE-209 adsorption, Zn-O_PET-CQDs and Fe-O_PET-CQDs exhibit the highest scattering intensities, suggesting strong interactions with the brominated flame retardant. Mg-O and Ca-G_PET-CQDs also show high intensities, indicating effective adsorption. Pristine PET-CQDs exhibit the lowest scattering only for sucralose, highlighting the role of metal doping in enhancing adsorption efficiency. Overall, the X-ray scattering analysis indicates that metal doping enhances the adsorption capacity of PET-CQDs. Fe and Zn doping are particularly effective for PFOS and BDE-209, while Ca and Mg doping improve adsorption for IBU and Sucralose.

The post adsorption MSD graphs display the molecular mobility of various metal-doped carbon quantum dots interacting with four pollutants over 100 picoseconds (Fig. S3). All systems exhibit increased MSD compared to the before adsorption (Fig. 4d) over time with a sharp uptick near the simulation end, indicating enhanced molecular mobility as time progresses[42]. For PFOS interactions, Ca-O_PET-CQDs exhibit the highest MSD value (18.3 Å2) while Ca-G_PET-CQDs show the lowest (9.6 Å2), indicating a significant influence of doping site on mobility. Literature on metal-doped nanomaterials, such as works by Ghosh et al [12] and Rao et al. [33], suggests that oxygen-bound dopants often enhance surface polarity and functionalization. This could facilitate stronger electrostatic attraction with polar pollutants like PFOS and may promote more dynamic reorientation compared to the graphene-like binding in Ca-G_PET-CQDs. When interacting with IBU, Zn-G_PET-CQDs demonstrate remarkably high molecular mobility (36.0 Å2), whereas Mg-O_PET-CQDs show much more limited movement (10.5 Å2). DFT studies by Nazir et al, [27] and Mollaamin and Monajjemi, [24] demonstrates that Zn dopants in graphene-like structures enhance electron density, agreeing with global softness data for the Zn-G_PET-CQDs (Table 1). This potentially weakens the van der Waals interactions with nonpolar regions of IBU, thus allowing greater translational freedom. Conversely, Mg-O doping, with its tendency to form stable coordination complexes[23], may anchor IBU more firmly, reducing mobility. For sucralose, Mg-G_PET-CQDs reach the highest MSD (17.1 Å2), while Mg-O_PET-CQDs exhibit the lowest value (4.8 Å2), highlighting how the same metal dopant at different sites can produce dramatically different dynamic behaviors. This could be that Mg dopants at graphene edges increased flexibility in adsorbate binding compared to oxygen-bound sites, which often stabilize small molecules like sucralose through hydrogen bonding. The difference within the same metal dopant emphasizes how structural context dictates dynamic behavior. In BDE-209 interactions, Ca-G_PET-CQDs show the highest mobility (33.0 Å2), contrasting sharply with Ca-O_PET-CQDs and Mg-O_PET-CQDs, which both display the lowest MSD values (5.7 Å2). These varying patterns across pollutants suggest that both the metal dopant and its specific binding site significantly influence the dynamic interactions between CQDs and different classes of environmental contaminants.

The Fig. 10 presents the diffusivity constants (D) of PET-CQDs and metal-doped PET-CQDs after adsorption and it was compared with the before adsorption (Fig. 5). The diffusivity constant reflects the mobility of CQDs in water, which can be influenced by structural modifications and interactions with pollutants[9,11]. A higher diffusivity constant indicates greater movement, while a lower value suggests restricted motion due to stronger pollutant binding or structural rigidity[9,11,44]. Before adsorption, PET-CQDs exhibit a low diffusivity constant of 0.00045, which increases after adsorption for most pollutants. This suggests that adsorption modifies surface properties and affects interactions with water molecules. For PFOS adsorption, the diffusivity of all CQDs increases after adsorption, with the highest increase observed in Ca-O_PET-CQDs (0.01268) and Fe-O_PET-CQDs (0.01145). This suggests that PFOS adsorption enhances CQD mobility, likely due to electrostatic interactions that alter surface properties. However, Zn-G_PET-CQDs (0.00368) and Fe-G_PET-CQDs (0.00485) show smaller increases, indicating stronger pollutant binding, which restricts movement. Ca-O_PET-CQDs (0.03482) exhibit the highest diffusivity after IBU adsorption, indicating weaker binding interactions that allow greater mobility. Fe-G_PET-CQDs (0.01353) and Fe-O_PET-CQDs (0.01198) also show significant increases, suggesting that Fe-doped CQDs remain highly mobile after IBU adsorption. In contrast, Mg-G_PET-CQDs (0.0062) and Zn-G_PET-CQDs (0.02803) show moderate increases, indicating that adsorption influences mobility differently based on the dopant. The diffusivity values remain relatively low for most CQDs, indicating strong pollutant interactions that restrict movement. Ca-O_PET-CQDs (0.00633) and Zn-O_PET-CQDs (0.00708) show slight increases, suggesting moderate adsorption strength that still allows some mobility. Mg-O_PET-CQDs (0.00173) exhibit the lowest diffusivity, indicating strong pollutant binding that leads to structural rigidity. However, the adsorption of BDE-209 significantly increases the diffusivity of Ca-G_PET-CQDs (0.02937) and Zn-G_PET-CQDs (0.01732). Fe-O_PET-CQDs (0.01152) and Fe-G_PET-CQDs (0.00485) show moderate increases, while Mg-G_PET-CQDs (0.00623) and Mg-O_PET-CQDs (0.00562) exhibit minimal diffusivity change, suggesting strong adsorption and structural stability. Generally, higher diffusivity after adsorption suggests weaker pollutant binding, allowing for greater movement. Ca-O and Fe-O PET-CQDs exhibit the highest mobility after PFOS and IBU adsorption, indicating weaker interactions. Mg-O and Fe-G PET-CQDs show the lowest diffusivity after Sucralose and BDE-209 adsorption, indicating stronger pollutant binding and higher adsorption efficiency. Furthermore, Fe-O-doped CQDs show balanced diffusivity across all pollutants, suggesting moderate adsorption that allows both effective pollutant interaction and reasonable mobility. These findings highlight that metal doping can be tailored to optimize PET-CQDs for specific pollutant adsorption applications, balancing the trade-offs between adsorption efficiency and structural mobility in environmental remediation.

The DACF power spectrum (Fig. 11 ) analyses reveal distinctive vibrational patterns for the CQDs interacting with different pollutants[9,11]. Comparing to before adsorption (Fig. 4f), disappearing, shifting and new peaks formed are due to the adsorption of the pollutants. For PFOS, the dominance of low-frequency peaks below 20 cm-1 across all CQDs suggests that interactions are primarily governed by soft vibrational modes, such as translational or librational motions of the pollutant relative to the CQD surface. This may be due to polar pollutants like PFOS inducing low-frequency oscillations in carbon nanostructures due to electrostatic interactions. The additional minor peaks between 20–40 cm-1 in Ca-G and Zn-G CQDs indicate secondary coupling mechanisms, possibly arising from metal-induced lattice distortions[1]. These dopants may enhance local electron density, facilitating weak vibrational interactions not seen in other systems. In contrast, the pristine PET-CQDs’ unique peak at 130 cm-1 points to a higher-frequency mode, potentially C–C stretching or bending, as undoped CQDs lack metal-mediated damping effects. This underscores a fundamental shift in interaction dynamics with doping. For IBU and BDE-209, the consistent low-frequency peaks below 20 cm-1 across all CQDs suggest a uniformity in adsorption mechanisms, likely driven by van der Waals forces or hydrophobic effects rather than dopant-specific influences. The low-frequency dominance in BDE-209 adsorption on carbon nanomaterials, can be attributed to the pollutant’s bulky, nonpolar nature limiting vibrational variability. This consistency implies that metal dopants play a less differentiating role for these contaminants, focusing adsorption on surface-area-driven interactions.

Sucralose interactions, however, reveal a standout case with Fe-G_PET-CQDs, where peaks extend up to 90 cm-1 alongside the ubiquitous < 20 cm-1 signals. This broader spectral range suggests multiple vibrational modes upon adsorption of sucralose, possibly including Fe-O or Fe-C stretching [13]. This unique fingerprint could reflect stronger coordination or hydrogen-bonding capabilities, boosting interaction efficiency. These findings highlight how metal doping not only enhances adsorption capacity but also fundamentally alters the vibrational characteristics of CQD-pollutant interactions, with specific metal dopants creating unique spectral signatures depending on the pollutant type.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed adsorption mechanisms for metal-doped PET-CQDS with various pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed adsorption mechanism of metal-doped PET-derived CQDs with PFOS, Ibuprofen (IBU), Sucralose, and BDE-209 is presented in Fig. 12 . The mechanisms influenced by various interactions, including ion–dipole interaction, electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, π–π stacking, hydrophobic interactions, and charge transfer mechanisms. The adsorption behavior depends generally on the type of metal dopant (Ca, Mg, Zn, Fe), and the chemical properties of the pollutant.

For PFOS adsorption (Fig. 12a), the negatively charged sulfonic acid (−SO3 -) functional group facilitates strong electrostatic interactions, C–C bonding hydrogen bonding (exhibited only with pristine PET-CQDs, confirmed by power spectra formed sharp peak at 130 cm−1, Fig. 11a), and charge transfer with pristine and doped PET-CQDs. Fe-doped CQDs (Fe-G and Fe-O) adsorption is driven by strong electrostatic attraction and enhanced charge transfer effects. The reduction of the HOMO-LUMO gap in Fe-G PET-CQDs (from 1.79 eV to 1.40 eV) improves electron delocalization, making adsorption more efficient. Zn-doped CQDs (Zn-G and Zn-O) bonded via hydrogen bonding and charge transfer between Zn’s surface functional groups and PFOS’s −SO3 - group. Additionally, Zn acts as a Lewis acid, further stabilizing pollutant interaction by π–π stacking interactions of the alkyl side groups [4]. Ca- and Mg-doped CQDs (Ca-G, Ca-O, Mg-G, Mg-O) facilitate adsorption mainly through ion–dipole interactions[21,2], where Ca2+ and Mg2+ interact with the fluorinated tail of PFOS leading to stable adsorption. For IBU adsorption (Fig. 12b), the presence of carboxyl (–COO-) and hydroxyl (–OH) functional groups allows for hydrogen bonding, π–π stacking, and electrostatic interactions with PET-CQDs. Fe-G and Fe-O PET-CQDs exhibited mainly hydrogen bonding and π–π interactions with the benzene ring in IBU via its graphitic PET-CQDs core, further stabilizing the adsorption. Zn-G and Zn-O PET-CQDs via as Zn forms coordination bonds with IBU’s oxygen-containing functional groups via electrostatic attraction furthermore, it formed hydrogen boding and π–π interactions with the benzene ring in IBU via its graphitic PET-CQDs core, further stabilizing the adsorption. Ca- and Mg-doped CQDs show weaker interactions, as adsorption mainly occurs through Ca2 + and Mg2+ coordinating with IBU's carboxylate (–COO-) group via electrostatic attraction (Vazquez [40]), stabilizing the pollutant on the CQD surface.

For Sucralose adsorption (Fig. 12c), the mechanism is primarily governed by hydrogen bonding and charge transfer interactions, due to its hydroxyl (–OH) and chlorine (−Cl) functional groups as well ion–dipole interactions. Mg-G and Zn-G PET-CQDs interacted mainly via hydrogen bonding between sucralose’s –OH groups and CQD surface functional groups. Ca-G and Ca-O PET-CQDs exhibited ion–dipole interactions between Ca2 + and the chlorine (−Cl) atoms in sucralose. Fe-doped CQDs show the weakest adsorption, as Fe doping enhances charge transfer, which is less effective for weakly charged molecules like sucralose. It further interacted via hydrogen bonding with the –OH groups. For BDE-209 adsorption (Fig. 12d), interactions occur mainly through π–π stacking, hydrophobic interactions, electrostatic and van der Waal forces, and charge transfer. Ca-O and Ca-G PET-CQDs show the strong adsorption, driven by hydrophobic interactions between the nonpolar brominated rings of BDE-209 and the graphitic CQD structure. Additionally, Ca2+ ions stabilize the interaction through weak electrostatic forces. Zn and Fe-doped PET-CQDs also demonstrate high adsorption efficiency, as charge transfer interactions between the brominated rings of BDE-209 and metal-doped CQDs enhance adsorption strength. Mg-G PET-CQDs adsorb BDE-209 through a combination of π–π stacking and van der Waals forces, further stabilizing pollutant interaction.

This study demonstrates the potential of dual-site doping of PET-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with alkali-earth (Ca, Mg) and transition (Zn, Fe) metals as a promising strategy for enhancing the adsorption of emerging toxic pollutants (EPs), including PFOS, ibuprofen (IBU), sucralose, and BDE-209. Computational analyses, including DFT and molecular dynamics simulations, reveal that metal doping at graphitic and carbonyl (C=O) sites significantly modifies the structural, electronic, and dynamic properties of PET-CQDs, tailoring their adsorption performance for diverse pollutant classes. Fe-doped CQDs (Fe-G and Fe-O) exhibit superior adsorption for PFOS and IBU, with binding energies reaching −5264.3 kcal/mol and −8209.88 kcal/mol, respectively, driven by enhanced electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and charge transfer facilitated by a reduced HOMO-LUMO gap (1.40 eV). BDE-209 displays the strongest adsorption across all CQDs (−23335 kcal/mol), attributed to π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, while sucralose shows limited affinity, with positive binding energies indicating weak interactions. Structural enhancements, such as increased surface area (e.g., 299.54 Å2 for Ca-O) and solvent accessibility, alongside metal-specific effects, underscore the tunability of PET-CQDs. Post-adsorption dynamics further highlight the balance between mobility and rigidity, with Ca-O and Zn-G CQDs showing high diffusivity (e.g., 0.03482 for IBU) and Fe-G and Mg-O CQDs demonstrating stronger pollutant retention. These findings establish key structure–property relationships, revealing that metal type and doping site can be strategically leveraged to optimize adsorption efficiency and selectivity. By valorizing PET waste into advanced adsorbents, this work aligns with circular economy principles and provides a foundation for designing sustainable, high-performance materials for water treatment, with potential for further refinement through multi-metal or heteroatom doping strategies to address a broader spectrum of environmental contaminants.
This research was supported by the Japanese Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS KAKENHI Award number: 24KF0131) through a Special Research Fellowship application.
